Electricity Theft Detection Method Based
on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional
Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement

Yifeng Yin1, Chang Liu1*, Yong Gan2, Yanhua Zhang1, and Qian Liang1
1 School of Computer Science and Technology, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry
Zhengzhou, Henan 450002, China
[e-mail: yinyifeng@zzuli.edu.cn, liuchang11125@163.com, yhzhang@email.zzuli.edu.cn,

published February 28, 2026

Abstract

In recent years, electricity theft has become a major issue affecting the stable operation of
smart grids. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of electricity theft detection is significantly
constrained by several factors. These include the high dimensionality and computational
intricacy of electricity data, complex behavioral patterns of electricity users, and the number
of electricity thieves far exceeds the number of normal users. For this reason, this paper first
proposes a multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based temporal feature extraction method to extract time-
series electricity usage features at different time scales in electricity usage data. Then, the
complementary enhancement of time-series features and statistical features of electricity
consumption data is realized by a cross-attention-driven multi-dimensional temporal feature
cross-enhancement method. Finally, an electricity theft detection model integrating CNN-
Mamba and multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement is constructed. Ablation
and comparative experiments were conducted on real-world power datasets. Through
experimental comparisons, statistical significance tests, model complexity analysis, and
deployment performance evaluations, this approach demonstrated significant effectiveness
and superiority in electricity theft detection.


Keywords: Cross-attention fusion, Electricity theft detection, Mamba, Multi-scale CNN,
Smart grid

1. Introduction
Recent studies indicate that the smart grid is an intelligent solution for energy monitoring,
and its emergence has effectively enhanced the energy efficiency, sustainability, security, and
reliability of the traditional grid [1], [2]. Non-Technical Losses (NTL) in smart grids refer to
electricity that’s allocated but not billed, and electricity theft is a main cause [3], [4]. Despite
the implementation of various measures by governments to prevent theft, electricity theft
continues to present a significant challenge [5]. This is because the way criminals steal
electricity has changed from the early days of changing the meter structure and private wiring
to the present day tampering with smart meter data [6], [7].
The advent of Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) has precipitated novel approaches
and challenges with regard to the detection of theft [8]. AMI can collect data on electricity
consumption and real-time electricity tariffs, but is also vulnerable to electricity theft. Through
the large amount of electric, meteorological, economic and other related data collected by
smart meters, researchers have proposed many data-driven based electricity theft detection
methods [9]. However, the performance of these methods is often limited by issues such as
high-dimensional user data, severe class imbalance, and a large number of outliers.
Furthermore, some methods rely on human resources, expensive hardware installations, or
comprehensive data assessments, which can result in high costs for power companies [10].
With the advancement of AI technologies, deep learning and machine learning have
demonstrated clear superiority in the field of electricity theft detection [11]. Classification is
the preferred method for artificial intelligence models to detect electricity theft. However, the
detection effectiveness of a single classification method is very limited [12].
The analysis reveals that the complexity of electricity theft manifests across two core
dimensions: First, the complexity of anomaly patterns encompasses both short-term localized
anomalies—such as sudden drops in instantaneous current—and long-term global anomalies
like sustained low power consumption over several months. The synergistic relationship
between these two patterns is key to distinguishing electricity theft from normal consumption
fluctuations. Second, the complexity of anomaly characteristics: time-series data reflects
dynamic evolution processes, while statistical features reveal static distribution patterns. The
complementary information from both is central to enhancing detection robustness. However,
existing methods suffer from irreconcilable limitations. First, single models cannot
simultaneously account for both local and global data modeling. Second, current feature fusion
approaches fail to adapt to heterogeneous information interactions. Third, models lack
customization and optimization for the specific characteristics of electricity theft behavior.
Consequently, there is still a pressing need to integrate advanced technologies with
innovative methods to further improve the effectiveness of power theft detection. Nowadays,
electric power companies collect a lot of historical information about power users’ electricity
consumption from smart meters, and there is an urgent need for a method that can more
accurately and efficiently analyze power users for power theft to help electric power
companies dig out the potential power theft users and reduce the economic losses of electric
power companies.
To address the aforementioned problems, this paper introduces Mamba into the processing
of electricity data for the first time, so that it works in concert with multiscale CNNs for time-
series feature extraction. Moreover, this paper fuses the temporal features and statistical
features with cross-attention to realize the complementary enhancement of the temporal

762 Yin et al.: Electricity Theft Detection Method
Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
features and statistical features, which enhances the model’s ability to capture the nonlinear
feature relationships in the electricity theft behavior.
The contributions of this work are generally demonstrated as such:
1) A multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based temporal feature extraction method is proposed. The
multi-scale CNN extracts temporal local features at different scales, enhancing the
detection capability of short-term anomalous user behavior. Combined with the time
series modeling network constructed by the enhanced Mamba model, it further captures
temporal dynamic patterns. By retaining key local information through learnable weights,
the model maintains Mamba’s low linear complexity when processing long sequences
while significantly improving sensitivity to local anomalies. The output of the multi-scale
CNN serves as the guiding feature for Mamba. Through residual connections, it is injected
into Mamba’s selective state space, directing Mamba’s dynamic weight adjustments to
focus on locally anomalous regions extracted by the CNN. This establishes a closed-loop
mechanism where local features guide global modeling. This temporal feature extraction
method effectively combines both local and global information from electricity
consumption data, enabling efficient learning of temporal patterns within complex user
electricity consumption behaviors.
2) A cross-attention-driven multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement method
is proposed. By introducing a cross-attention mechanism, it efficiently fuses temporal
features extracted by multi-scale CNN-Mamba and statistical features, enabling
comprehensive multi-dimensional temporal feature interaction. A bidirectional retrieval
approach is constructed, dynamically retrieving statistical features corresponding to local
anomalies while simultaneously back-tracking to locate the temporal intervals associated
with statistical anomalies. This achieves complementary enhancement between temporal
and statistical features, addressing the limitations of single-input approaches in traditional
methods. This approach comprehensively captures users’ electricity consumption
patterns while adapting to heterogeneous feature interactions. It possesses the capability
to guide semantic alignment and dynamically weighted fusion among multidimensional
temporal features, achieving effective integration of users’ multidimensional electricity
consumption temporal characteristics.
3) By integrating multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based temporal feature extraction with cross-
attention-based multidimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement, this approach
conducts end-to-end customized optimization at the data, feature, and modeling levels to
address the characteristics of electricity theft. This results in the construction of an
electricity theft detection model based on CNN-Mamba and multidimensional temporal
feature cross-enhancement.
4) This paper conducts ablation and comparative experiments on the publicly available real-
world user electricity consumption dataset from State Grid Corporation of China (SGCC).
Furthermore, this paper demonstrates that the proposed method achieves robust and
efficient electricity theft detection through performance evaluations encompassing
statistical significance tests, model complexity quantification, and deployment testing.
The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 discusses the related work
on electricity theft detection research, and Section 3 presents the method and model. The
experimental results and their subsequent analysis are detailed in Section 4. Conclusively,
Section 5 offers a synopsis of the entire paper. The overview of this paper is shown in Fig. 1.

2. Related Work
Recent developments in data science and machine learning have led to methods that use data-
driven to detect electricity theft [13], [14]. In [15], the width convolutional neural network
(CNN) part is used to capture the global features of the power consumption data while the
depth CNN part is used to capture the periodicity of the power consumption data. The article
evaluates the performance of the proposed model using the area under the ROC curve (AUC)
and mean average precision (MAP), but does not address the imbalance in the distribution of
user classes. In [16], the authors analyzed daily power consumption data using a Long Short-
Term Memory (LSTM) network, while non-sequential data was processed using a Multi-Layer
Perceptron (MLP) network. However, the authors did not address the problem of unbalanced
data classes and the model has poor generalization ability. In [17], the authors employ CNN
to extract features from time-series data and subsequently use LSTM for classification.
However, the use of only the SMOTE [18] technique to mitigate data imbalance in the article
led to class overlap issues. In [19], a SOMTE algorithm is proposed based on K-means [20]
improvement to balance the dataset and finally uses Random Forest (RF) for classification.
Nevertheless, as the number of decision trees increases, the RF algorithm may encounter
bottlenecks when processing high-dimensional data. In [21], the authors selected and extracted
features through CNN followed by classification using GRUs optimized by Particle Swarm
Optimization (PSO) algorithm. However, since these models are integrated in series, the
computational cost of detection is relatively high. In [22], the authors use SMOTETomek [23]
technique to balance the data, extract and select the most relevant features via the FRESH [24]

764 Yin et al.: Electricity Theft Detection Method
Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
algorithm, and finally utilize CatBoost to complete the classification task. However, since the
model did not optimize the hyperparameters, the classification performance only reached a
local optimum. In [25], deep CNN were used to detect smart meter consumption data, but due
to the fact that CNN loses some of the contextual information when processing time-series
data, it is not conducive to the identification of time-trend-based electricity theft. In [26], the
authors input both the original one-dimensional power data and the processed two-dimensional
power data into the CNN model for feature processing, and add XGBoost to the CNN’s output
layer as a classifier. However, the authors neglected the problem of dealing with unbalanced
data classes. In [9], the authors proposed a temporal residual 3D CNN architecture that extends
the receptive field through 3D convolutional kernels and mitigates gradient vanishing via
residual connections, aiming to capture spatio-temporal correlation anomalies in long
sequences. However, the substantial number of parameters in 3D CNNs increases training time,
rendering it unsuitable for the real-time monitoring requirements of power companies. In [27],
the authors propose the V-LSTM framework, which employs a Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
to reduce the dimensionality of high-dimensional time-series data, followed by an LSTM to
model long-term sequence dependencies, thereby enabling real-time anomaly detection in
distributed scenarios. However, the VAE only extracts low-dimensional features from the
time-series data without integrating statistical features. Furthermore, the LSTM’s feature
analysis relies on unidirectional time-series modeling, lacking dynamic interaction
mechanisms, and still suffers from gradient vanishing issues when processing long sequences.
In summary, most of the existing research methods for power theft detection extract the
complex patterns of power theft from time series data, and lack the ability to analyze the power
consumption characteristics of power users from multiple levels. Furthermore, existing studies
suffer from low efficiency in processing long sequences of electricity consumption data.
3. Method
3.1 Multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based Feature Extraction
Simple CNN can only extract limited information, so this paper introduces multi-scale CNN
to extract multilevel information by expanding convolution at different scales [28]. This paper
proposes a method for feature extraction using multi-scale CNN, as shown in Fig. 2.
The multiscale CNN feature extraction part of the temporal feature extraction method
proposed in this paper consists of three heterogeneous branches.
1) Local fine-grained feature extraction
Branch 1 is local fine-grained feature extraction, which uses a combination of three layers
of ordinary convolution and dilated convolution to capture local detailed features and
temporal patterns with medium receptive fields. It is suitable for detecting short-term
abnormal fluctuations in power consumption data, such as sudden drops in instantaneous
current.
2) Deep feature extraction
Branch 2 is for deep feature extraction, using deep separable convolutions, i.e., low-rank
decomposition of standard convolution kernel tensors. By separating spatial and channel
correlations, it improves efficiency and reduces the number of parameters while
maintaining a large receptive field. Deep separable convolutions can efficiently capture
medium-range time dependencies, making them suitable for detecting periodic power
consumption patterns such as the start and stop of regular power-consuming equipment.


KSII TRANSACTIONS ON INTERNET AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS VOL. 20, NO. 2, February 2026 765
3) Hierarchical feature extraction
Branch 3 is hierarchical feature extraction, which uses a large convolutional kernel with
a hierarchical mechanism, focusing on a certain context, to extract long-period power
usage patterns such as continuous low power operation.


Fig. 2. Architecture of multi-scale CNN.

The multi-scale CNN extracts the basic temporal features of user electricity consumption
data by fusing the multi-scale features of the three branches via convolution, thereby realizing
the adaptive weighting of multi-scale semantic information and significantly enhancing the
capture of dynamic patterns. In order to capture the global temporal dependency in user
electricity consumption data and break through the receptive field limitation of CNNs in long-
sequence modeling, this method adopts the enhanced Mamba model for fine-grained temporal
sequential modeling of the features extracted by the multi-scale CNN, forming a hierarchical
feature learning from local to global.
Existing studies generally rely on RNN or Transformer [29] for time series modeling, but
there are problems of high computational complexity or insufficient long sequence modeling
capability. Mamba model originates from the improvement of Structured State Space Models
(S4), whose core goal is to solve the efficiency bottleneck problem of traditional sequence
models when dealing with long sequences [30]. S4 is to map a one-dimensional sequence

The enhanced Mamba time series modeling model adds a residual connection design to
preserve the original timing information compared to the traditional Mamba, and sets
normalization after residual join to normalize the output after residual join. The model can
realize dynamic focusing at key time points to target the intermittent anomalous characteristics
of electricity theft through the selective state mechanism, and maintain linear complexity when

The multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based temporal feature extraction method captures local
multi-scale features by using multi-scale CNN, while the enhanced Mamba model is
responsible for modeling global time series dependency. This complementary design
overcomes the defect of a single model that is difficult to take into account both the local
details and global laws, which can be used for realizing the high-precision electricity theft
detection task. which lays a solid foundation for realizing high-precision power theft detection.
3.2 Cross-attention-driven Feature Cross-enhancement
Compared with simple feature splicing or summing with fixed weights, the cross-attention
mechanism is able to dynamically focus on anomaly-related features and adjust the weights in
a data-driven manner, which is more suitable for capturing complex nonlinear feature
relationships in electricity theft behavior [35]. The multi-head attention mechanism enables
the model to capture semantic correlations across different subspaces in parallel. Mamba’s
temporal features serve as the Query, while statistical features act as both Key and Value.
Through cross-attention calculations, Mamba’s temporal features actively retrieve
complementary information from statistical features, generating a weighted fusion of feature
representations to achieve feature interaction.
Since the query vector and key/value vectors possess different sequence dimensions, linear
projection is first applied to unify the feature dimensions to

Finally, the outputs from the eight heads are concatenated along the last dimension and
then compressed to the target dimension via linear projection. This achieves the fusion of
multi-subspace information and dimension alignment. Residual connections are introduced to
preserve the original temporal feature information, while layer normalization stabilizes the
training gradient, forming a complete feature fusion chain. Additionally, the residual learning
mechanism introduced during feature fusion dynamically adjusts the contribution of attention
outputs through learnable scaling parameters. This approach preserves original temporal
features while adaptively regulating the intensity of feature fusion.
In addition, the residual learning mechanism is introduced during feature fusion adaptively
adjust the feature fusion strength while retaining the original time-series features. After fusing
temporal and statistical features via cross-attention, our method applies attention pooling to
compress the interacted temporal features into a global feature vector. This step identifies the
critical time points corresponding to anomalous periods of theft behavior along the temporal
dimension, suppresses the influence of noisy intervals, and enhances feature robustness. The
cross-attention-driven multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement method is
illustrated in Fig. 4.
The cross-attention-driven multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement method
utilizes the cross-attention mechanism to deeply fuse heterogeneous features, and achieves
global compression of isomorphic temporal features through attention pooling, and the
collaboration between the two achieves progressive information refinement from “local
feature interactions” to “global feature representation”. In addition, the method realizes the

3.3 Proposed Model
The proposed method for constructing a model is based on CNN-Mamba and multi-
dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement, the model is designed with four parts: data
preprocessing, feature extraction, feature fusion, and classification, and the model structure is
shown in Fig. 5.
1) Data Preprocessing
Linear interpolation combined with forward and backward padding is first taken to fill in
the missing values. Then, three-sigma rule [36] is utilized to process the outliers in the dataset.
Moreover, a combination of stratified sampling and SMOTEENN [37] to mitigate dataset
imbalances.
2) Feature Extraction
The model uses a two-branch input of temporal and statistical features for feature extraction.
The temporal features are derived from the preprocessed electricity consumption data, while

770 Yin et al.: Electricity Theft Detection Method
Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
the statistical features are input from the statistical features extracted from the preprocessed
electricity consumption data using the FRESH and Boruta [38] algorithms. The CNN-Mamba
model extracts multi-level temporal features using temporal data. At the same time, the
statistical features are processed in a fully connected layer, which maps high-dimensional
features to low-dimensional space while providing an aligned dimension for feature fusion.
3) Feature Fusion
The temporal features and statistical features are cross-attention fused to realize the
interaction of multi-dimensional temporal features. The multidimensional time-series features
after feature interaction are input into the attention pooling layer to adaptively focus on
abnormal time periods through learnable global query vectors to deal with non-uniformly
distributed electricity theft features.
4) Classification
The final classification layer of the model is responsible for the higher-order abstraction of
the fused features, and compresses the high-dimensional feature space into interpretable
dimensions through two segmented linear dimensionality reduction, avoiding the common
dimensionality explosion problem in electricity theft detection. Finally, the classification layer
converts one-dimensional logits into electricity theft probability through sigmoid for
classification.


Fig. 5. Architecture of the proposed model.

4. Experiments and analysis
In this section, experiments are conducted using the SGCC’s publicly available real user
electricity consumption dataset to evaluate the performance of the electricity theft detection
method proposed in this paper. First, the experimental setup is described, followed by an
introduction to the evaluation metrics. Finally, this paper validates the effectiveness of the
proposed method through ablation studies and comparative experiments. It conducts multi-
level analyses, including statistical significance tests, model complexity assessments, and
deployment performance evaluations, to demonstrate the method’s advanced capabilities and
deployability.
4.1 Experimental setup
In this paper, the electricity consumption data of real users made public by SGCC in China
was used, and the electricity consumption data for a total of 1035 days from January 1, 2014
to October 31, 2016 were collected in the dataset, with a sampling frequency of once a day.
The dataset contains a total of 42,372 power users’ electricity consumption data and the
corresponding labels of whether or not they are power theft users, among which there are
38,757 normal users and 3,615 theft users, and the theft users only account for 8.53% of the
total power users, which leads to a serious category imbalance in the dataset, which seriously
affects the training of the model and the effectiveness of power theft detection. In addition, the
dataset contains a large number of missing values and outliers, which need to be further
processed.
1) Missing value imputation
After counting, the dataset contains 25.64% missing values. Since the consumer electricity
consumption data is linear, a linear interpolation combined with forward padding and

3) Dataset partitioning
The original electricity dataset suffers from severe category imbalance, which distorts the
decision boundary and makes the model tend to predict the samples as majority classes.
Therefore, the dataset in this study is partitioned using stratified sampling, with 80% of the
electricity consumption dataset as the training set and 20% as the test set. Stratified sampling
avoids the minority class samples from being flooded during training by enforcing the
retention of the proportion of abnormal samples in the training set, giving the model a chance
to learn its patterns.
4) Time Window Division
Considering the daily sampling frequency of the SGCC dataset and the periodic
characteristics of electricity consumption, an overlapping window segmentation strategy with
a fixed window length of 90 days and a sliding step size of 30 days is adopted. Each user’s
complete 1035-day time series within the training set is divided into consecutive overlapping
windows, generating 32 valid windows per user. Data within each window participates as an
independent sample in feature extraction. The 30-day sliding step ensures continuity between
adjacent windows, preventing disruption of critical temporal patterns. This strategy was
chosen because electricity consumption exhibits pronounced quarterly periodicity. The 90-day
window fully covers a quarterly consumption cycle, capturing seasonal theft patterns while
balancing long-term pattern detection with computational complexity control. The 30-day
overlap step reduces boundary effects at window edges, enhancing temporal feature
continuity—particularly suited for capturing the intermittent nature of electricity theft. For
each user in the test set, window segmentation strictly adheres to the training set’s window
parameters without adjusting any segmentation rules. This ensures consistent generation logic
between test and training set windows while maintaining complete data independence. After
segmentation, training set window samples are used for model training, while test set window
samples are used for performance evaluation, preventing cross-over of temporal information
across datasets.
5) Automated statistical feature extraction
In order to further improve the ability to recognize power theft users, this study needs to
extract statistical features from power users’ electricity consumption data. Traditional manual
statistical feature extraction methods are highly subjective, for this reason, this paper combines
the FRESH algorithm and the Boruta algorithm to realize the automated and efficient
extraction of statistical features of power users’ electricity consumption data.
FRESH [24] is a feature engineering framework for time series classification and regression
tasks, and its core idea is to screen the features that are significantly correlated with the target
variables through scalable statistical hypothesis tests. The core idea is to filter the features that
are strongly correlated with the target variables through scalable statistical hypothesis tests.
TS-Fresh is a Python library for implementing the FRESH algorithm, which replaces the
traditional manual feature engineering with systematic algorithms to automatically extract
hundreds of statistical features, and filter the features that are strongly correlated with the target
variables through hypothesis tests. Through the TS-Fresh filtering mechanism, 777 temporal
features were extracted, providing rich temporal information. However, further feature
selection is still required to reduce the dimensionality of the features, as the rationality of
feature selection fundamentally impacts the accuracy of classification results.
Boruta [37] is a wraparound feature selection method based on random forests, which
generates shadow features by randomly perturbing the original features to construct an
augmented dataset containing both original and shadow features. The features generated by
TS-Fresh may contain noisy features that are accidentally related to the target variables, Boruta

KSII TRANSACTIONS ON INTERNET AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS VOL. 20, NO. 2, February 2026 773
can effectively filter these pseudo-relevant features through self-service resampling and
shadow feature comparison. Since lag effects and multicollinearity often exist in time-series
data, Boruta can identify nonlinear relationships between features, and its random forest base
algorithm is naturally robust to non-normal distributions and outliers of time-series features,
making it more suitable for processing time-series data than variance filtering or mutual
information methods.
After filtering via the Boruta algorithm, 64 core statistical features were ultimately retained.
These retained features primarily serve as sensitive indicators for electricity theft behavior,
including: number of short-term load fluctuations, average daily load fluctuation coefficient,
proportion of nighttime load, quarterly load peak offset, autocorrelation coefficient, and
proportion of low-frequency components in power spectral density. These features effectively
distinguish differences in electricity consumption patterns between normal users and
electricity thieves. To ensure model generalization capability, the principle of “independent
training set processing with inherited test set parameters” was strictly adhered to. TS-Fresh
statistical feature extraction and Boruta feature selection were performed exclusively on the
training set. Feature extraction for the test set strictly reused the training set’s parameters
without calculating any statistical parameters for the test set independently. This ensured the
test set remained completely independent from the training process, preventing information
leakage.

7) SMOTEENN
Three common sample balancing algorithms in electricity theft detection are oversampling,
undersampling and resampling, among which the SMOTE [18] oversampling algorithm is
widely accepted. Specifically, the SMOTE algorithm performs linear interpolation in the
feature space of the minority class samples to generate new samples that are consistent with
the distribution of the original data, thus balancing the category proportions. ENN (Edited
Nearest Neighbors) is an intelligent undersampling algorithm based on the nearest neighbor
rule, which aims to optimize the distribution of the categories and enhance the performance of
the classifiers by removing the noisy samples and boundary fuzzy samples. classifier
performance.
SMOTEENN [36] is a hybrid resampling technique that combines SMOTE oversampling
and ENN undersampling techniques. The core mechanism of SMOTEENN is divided into a
SMOTE oversampling phase and an ENN undersampling phase. The SMOTE oversampling
phase first interpolates the minority class samples to generate synthetic samples, avoiding
overfitting caused by simple repetitive samples, and at the same time enhancing the
distribution density of the minority class samples. The ENN undersampling stage eliminates
noisy samples generated by SMOTE, while pruning samples with blurred boundaries between

4.2 Evaluation indexes
In order to better evaluate the performance of this method, the confusion matrix of power theft
detection is firstly established. TP indicates that the power theft users predicted by the power
theft detection method are the same as their real results, and they are all power theft users. FN
indicates that the real results are power theft users, but the power theft detection method
predicts the results to be normal users, which is an error that directly causes economic loss and
may condone the long-term power theft detection behavior. FP denotes a sample of normal
users with true results but predicted by the stealing detection method to be a stealing user, and
this type of error causes the utility to waste resources to verify the results.TN denotes a sample
of normal users whose true results are the same as those predicted by the stealing detection
method. The indexes to be prioritized in electricity theft detection are: accuracy, recall,
precision, F1-score, AUC value, and these evaluation indexes are calculated based on the
confusion matrix.

4.3 Ablation Experiment
To validate the effectiveness of each module in the electricity theft detection method proposed
in this study, this section designed the following five ablation experiments to compare their
performance. The specific hyperparameter settings for the model are shown in Table 1, and
the experimental overview is presented in Table 2. The data comparison of the metrics of
different ablation experiments is shown in Table 3, and a more intuitive metrics comparison
graph is shown in Fig. 7.
1) Verify the effectiveness of cross-attention
The cross-attention mechanism is removed in favor of simple splicing for feature fusion.
Simple concatenation only connects feature vectors in dimension, without complex weight
calculations or transformations, so all input feature information is fully retained. However,
simple concatenation lacks active modeling of nonlinear relationships between features,
resulting in the loss of interaction information between time-series dynamic features and

776 Yin et al.: Electricity Theft Detection Method
Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
statistical features, and insufficient learning of key pattern correlations. The loss of the
dynamic allocation of weights by the cross-attention mechanism makes the model suffer from
the interference of irrelevant features in the detection of electricity theft, and reduces the
sensitivity to weakly correlated features. The experimental results show that the performance
of the model in all aspects decreases to a certain extent after changing the cross-attention
mechanism to simple concatenation, in which the recall and AUC values decrease by more
than 5%.
2) Validation of Mamba time series modeling
Changing Mamba time series modeling to ordinary convolution makes the electricity theft
detection model lose the long-range modeling advantage of the state space model, and the local
receptive field of convolution is difficult to capture the long-term dependence of electricity
theft behavior. The experimental results show that the model performance in all aspects after
changing the Mamba time series modeling to ordinary convolution has a relatively large
decrease, in which the recall and F1-score decrease is more than 7%.
3) Validating the Performance Advantages of the Mamba Module
Replacing the enhanced Mamba model with a standard parameter-aligned Transformer
encoder resulted in significant declines across all core metrics. AUC, recall, precision, and
accuracy all decreased by over 3%, demonstrating that Mamba’s selective state space
mechanism and low linear complexity are irreplaceable for electricity theft detection tasks. In
contrast, replacing Mamba’s time-series modeling with standard convolutional layers in
ablation experiments yielded significant improvements across all metrics, further highlighting
Mamba’s performance advantage in modeling long sequences.
4) Validate the effectiveness of multi-scale CNN
Removing the multi-scale CNN in favor of using Mamba alone for time series data. This
results in a model that retains the ability to model time series, but is difficult to capture key
local information of the time series data due to the lack of multi-scale convolution for local
feature enhancement. The experimental results show that the model decreases more than 2.5%
in three aspects: accuracy, recall, and AUC value.
5) Verify the effectiveness of the input statistical features for model performance
enhancement
Only time-series data is input into the model to extract the time-series features of
consumption data for user classification, which can lead to the loss of key statistical indicators
such as volatility and periodicity in the statistical features, weakening the interpretability of
anomalous electricity consumption patterns. The experimental results show that the model
performance decreases approximately 4% to 6% in all aspects.
6) Verify the effectiveness of the time-series features extracted by the model for model
performance enhancement
Inputting only the statistical features extracted by the automated statistical feature
extraction method in the model results in the model’s inability to more accurately capture the
user’s electricity theft patterns, as the fine-grained temporal patterns in the original time-series
data cannot be fully preserved by the statistical features. The experimental results show that
compared to other ablation experimental methods, inputting only statistical features makes the
model’s performance in all aspects of the model show the most significant decrease, which is
more than 7%, with the recall rate, F1-score, and AUC value decreasing by more than 8%.

4.4 Comparison Experiment
The five high-performance electricity theft detection methods mentioned in Section 2 were
compared in the same experimental environment using the same pre-processed data set to
demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method. The comparison method is as follows:
1) RF
RF [19] is a machine learning algorithm based on ensemble learning. Its core idea is to
construct a set of multiple decision trees and use a majority voting mechanism to combine the
prediction results of all trees for classification tasks. The algorithm significantly improves the
accuracy and robustness of the classification task by integrating differentiated decision trees,
combined with Bootstrap sampling and feature randomization.
2) CNN-LSTM
The CNN-LSTM [17] electricity theft detection model combines CNN and LSTM. CNN
first extracts features and reduces the dimensionality of the data, removing redundant
information from the data, and then uses LSTM to further process and learn the extracted
feature sequences. This integrated architecture shows high efficiency and robustness in
handling smart grid data classification tasks.
3) CNN-PSO-GRU
The hybrid electricity theft detection model based on CNN-PSO-GRU [15] uses CNN for
feature extraction, GRU for processing sequential data, and PSO for optimizing GRU
parameters, thereby enhancing the model’s generalization capabilities and addressing
overfitting issues.
4) CatBoost
CatBoost [16] is a machine learning algorithm that is particularly suitable for handling
datasets containing a large number of categorical features. While traditional machine learning
models usually need to process categorical features with unique hot coding resulting in
increased data dimensions and computational complexity, CatBoost can directly and
efficiently process categorical features, reducing data preprocessing effort and potential
information loss. In addition, CatBoost’s built-in regularization technology can effectively
prevent overfitting of the model, so that it can maintain good generalization performance in
the face of complex data, and avoid the problem of poor detection results in practical
applications due to overfitting of training data.
5) Deep CNN
In dealing with the problem of electricity theft detection, Deep CNN [25] can automatically
capture the nonlinear correlations in the electricity usage patterns and adapt to the diversified
means of electricity theft, which has become the core technologies in electricity theft detection
methods.

A multidimensional comparison of model complexity reveals that RF is lightweight and
efficient with low deployment costs, but exhibits weak modeling capabilities for high-
dimensional time-series data. CatBoost handles categorical features efficiently and resists
overfitting, yet struggles to capture dependencies in long sequences. Deep CNN excels at local
feature extraction but suffers from significant context information loss and redundant
parameters. CNN-LSTM excels in time-series modeling, but suffers from LSTM gradient
vanishing, low efficiency in handling long sequences, and high parameter count. CNN-PSO-
GRU improves generalization after parameter optimization, but its cascaded structure causes
computational redundancy and limited throughput. The proposed method significantly
surpasses baseline models in terms of capacity density, achieving an optimal balance between
detection performance and computational efficiency. Cross-attention fusion enhances

4.7 Deploy Performance Testing
To test whether this model meets the deployment requirements of power companies, this
subsection designs an analysis of practical deployability. All tests are based on the
preprocessed SGCC dataset, with models utilizing FP32 precision and edge devices employing
INT8 quantization optimization. Latency measurements represent the average ± standard
deviation of 1,000 independent experiments, while throughput testing follows batch
processing specifications commonly used in the power industry. When deployed on standard
x86 servers, the Proposed Model incurs an average annual deployment cost of 0.008 yuan per
user. Calculating based on an average annual electricity theft of 1000 kWh per user, an
electricity rate of 0.6 yuan/kWh, and an additional revenue of 600 yuan per newly detected
user, the 1-2% performance improvement from deploying this model is significantly smaller
than the electricity cost savings. Furthermore, edge deployment eliminates the need for
additional cloud transmission links, significantly reducing communication costs.
Performance testing demonstrates that the proposed model delivers low single-user
inference latency (58.2±4.3 ms on x86 servers) and high batch throughput (22.3 users/second),
fully satisfying the practical demands of power companies for both daily batch inspections and
real-time anomaly monitoring. It also achieves substantial improvements in batch throughput
efficiency compared to the CNN-LSTM baseline model, supporting high-frequency

Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
5. Conclusion
This paper proposes a method that for the first time combines a CNN-Mamba hybrid model
with multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-attention fusion, constructing a framework of
“multi-scale local sensing-global temporal modeling-heterogeneous feature fusion” for
electricity theft detection. Unlike the traditional single-dimensional approach, this method
simultaneously models the depth of spatio-temporal features of time series and the global
semantic characterization of statistical features, and breaks through the performance
bottleneck of the traditional static fusion method through the theory of dynamic fusion of
multidimensional time series features. Through ablation and comparative experiments, along
with a series of analytical results, it has been collectively confirmed that the proposed
framework enables reliable and efficient electricity theft detection. Its performance
improvements translate into tangible benefits for grid operation and reduced losses. Future
work may integrate the federated learning framework to achieve dynamic optimization while
ensuring privacy protection.

Abstract

In recent years, electricity theft has become a major issue affecting the stable operation of
smart grids. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of electricity theft detection is significantly
constrained by several factors. These include the high dimensionality and computational
intricacy of electricity data, complex behavioral patterns of electricity users, and the number
of electricity thieves far exceeds the number of normal users. For this reason, this paper first
proposes a multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based temporal feature extraction method to extract time-
series electricity usage features at different time scales in electricity usage data. Then, the
complementary enhancement of time-series features and statistical features of electricity
consumption data is realized by a cross-attention-driv

ferent time scales in electricity usage data. Then, the
complementary enhancement of time-series features and statistical features of electricity
consumption data is realized by a cross-attention-driven multi-dimensional temporal feature
cross-enhancement method. Finally, an electricity theft detection model integrating CNN-
Mamba and multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement is constructed. Ablation
and comparative experiments were conducted on real-world power datasets. Through
experimental comparisons, statistical significance tests, model complexity analysis, and
deployment performance evaluations, this approach demonstrated significant effectiveness
and superiority in electricity theft detection.

Recent studies indicate that the smart grid is an intelligent solution for energy monitoring,
and its emergence has effectively enhanced the energy efficiency, sustainability, security, and
reliability of the traditional grid [1], [2]. Non-Technical Losses (NTL) in smart grids refer to
electricity that’s allocated but not billed, and electricity theft is a main cause [3], [4]. Despite
the implementation of various measures by governments to prevent theft, electricity theft
continues to present a significant challenge [5]. This is because the way criminals steal
electricity has changed from the early days of changing the meter structure and private wiring
to the present day tampering with smart meter data [6], [7].
The advent of Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) has precipitated novel approaches
and challenges with regard to the detection of theft [8]. AMI

The advent of Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) has precipitated novel approaches
and challenges with regard to the detection of theft [8]. AMI can collect data on electricity
consumption and real-time electricity tariffs, but is also vulnerable to electricity theft. Through
the large amount of electric, meteorological, economic and other related data collected by
smart meters, researchers have proposed many data-driven based electricity theft detection
methods [9]. However, the performance of these methods is often limited by issues such as
high-dimensional user data, severe class imbalance, and a large number of outliers.
Furthermore, some methods rely on human resources, expensive hardware installations, or
comprehensive data assessments, which can result in high costs for power companies [10].

the preferred method for artificial intelligence models to detect electricity theft. However, the
detection effectiveness of a single classification method is very limited [12].
The analysis reveals that the complexity of electricity theft manifests across two core
dimensions: First, the complexity of anomaly patterns encompasses both short-term localized
anomalies—such as sudden drops in instantaneous current—and long-term global anomalies
like sustained low power consumption over several months. The synergistic relationship
between these two patterns is key to distinguishing electricity theft from normal consumption
fluctuations. Second, the complexity of anomaly characteristics: time-series data reflects

existing methods suffer from irreconcilable limitations. First, single models cannot
simultaneously account for both local and global data modeling. Second, current feature fusion
approaches fail to adapt to heterogeneous information interactions. Third, models lack
customization and optimization for the specific characteristics of electricity theft behavior.
Consequently, there is still a pressing need to integrate advanced technologies with
innovative methods to further improve the effectiveness of power theft detection. Nowadays,

3.1 Multi-scale CNN-Mamba-based Feature Extraction
Simple CNN can only extract limited information, so this paper introduces multi-scale CNN
to extract multilevel information by expanding convolution at different scales [28]. This paper
proposes a method for feature extraction using multi-scale CNN, as shown in Fig. 2.
The multiscale CNN feature extraction part of the temporal feature extraction method
proposed in this paper consists of three heterogeneous branches.
1) Local fine-grained feature extraction
Branch 1 is local fine-grained feature extraction, which uses a combination of three layers
of ordinary convolution and dilated convolution to capture local detailed features and
temporal patterns with medium receptive fields. It is suitable for detecting short-term
abnormal fluctuations in power consumption data, such as su

d convolution to capture local detailed features and
temporal patterns with medium receptive fields. It is suitable for detecting short-term
abnormal fluctuations in power consumption data, such as sudden drops in instantaneous
current.
2) Deep feature extraction
Branch 2 is for deep feature extraction, using deep separable convolutions, i.e., low-rank
decomposition of standard convolution kernel tensors. By separating spatial and channel
correlations, it improves efficiency and reduces the number of parameters while
maintaining a large receptive field. Deep separable convolutions can efficiently capture
medium-range time dependencies, making them suitable for detecting periodic power
consumption patterns such as the start and stop of regular power-consuming equipment.

The proposed method for constructing a model is based on CNN-Mamba and multi-
dimensional temporal feature cross-enhancement, the model is designed with four parts: data
preprocessing, feature extraction, feature fusion, and classification, and the model structure is
shown in Fig. 5.
1) Data Preprocessing
Linear interpolation combined with forward and backward padding is first taken to fill in
the missing values. Then, three-sigma rule [36] is utilized to process the outliers in the dataset.
Moreover, a combination of stratified sampling and SMOTEENN [37] to mitigate dataset
imbalances.
2) Feature Extraction
The model uses a two-branch input of temporal and statistical features for feature extraction.

3) Feature Fusion
The temporal features and statistical features are cross-attention fused to realize the

3) Feature Fusion
The temporal features and statistical features are cross-attention fused to realize the

after feature interaction are input into the attention pooling layer to adaptively focus on
abnormal time periods through learnable global query vectors to deal with non-uniformly
distributed electricity theft features.

The final classification layer of the model is responsible for the higher-order abstraction of
the fused features, and compresses the high-dimensional feature space into interpretable
dimensions through two segmented linear dimensionality reduction, avoiding the common
dimensionality explosion problem in electricity theft detection. Finally, the classification layer
converts one-dimensional logits into electricity theft probability through sigmoid for
classification.

3) Dataset partitioning
The original electricity dataset suffers from severe category imbalance, which distorts the
decision boundary and makes the model tend to predict the samples as majority classes.
Therefore, the dataset in this study is partitioned using stratified sampling, with 80% of the
electricity consumption dataset as the training set and 20% as the test set. Stratified sampling
avoids the minority class samples from being flooded during training by enforcing the
retention of

the
electricity consumption dataset as the training set and 20% as the test set. Stratified sampling
avoids the minority class samples from being flooded during training by enforcing the
retention of the proportion of abnormal samples in the training set, giving the model a chance
to learn its patterns.
4) Time Window Division
Considering the daily sampling frequency of the SGCC dataset and the periodic
characteristics of electricity consumption, an overlapping window segmentation strategy with
a fixed window length of 90 days and a sliding step size of 30 days is adopted. Each user’s
complete 1035-day time series within the training set is divided into consecutive overlapping
windows, generating 32 valid windows per user. Data within each window participates as an
independent sample in feature extraction. The 30-day sliding step ensures continuity between
adjacent windows, preventing disruption of critical temporal patterns. This strategy was
chosen because electricity consumption exh

ature extraction. The 30-day sliding step ensures continuity between
adjacent windows, preventing disruption of critical temporal patterns. This strategy was
chosen because electricity consumption exhibits pronounced quarterly periodicity. The 90-day
window fully covers a quarterly consumption cycle, capturing seasonal theft patterns while
balancing long-term pattern detection with computational complexity control. The 30-day
overlap step reduces boundary effects at window edges, enhancing temporal feature
continuity—particularly suited for capturing the intermittent nature of electricity theft. For
each user in the test set, window segmentation strictly adheres to the training set’s window
parameters without adjusting any segmentation rules. This ensures consistent generation logic
between test and training set windows while maintaining complete data independence. After
segmentation, training set window samples are used for model training, while test set window
samples are used for pe

n test and training set windows while maintaining complete data independence. After
segmentation, training set window samples are used for model training, while test set window
samples are used for performance evaluation, preventing cross-over of temporal information
across datasets.
5) Automated statistical feature extraction
In order to further improve the ability to recognize power theft users, this study needs to
extract statistical features from power users’ electricity consumption data. Traditional manual
statistical feature extraction methods are highly subjective, for this reason, this paper combines
the FRESH algorithm and the Boruta algorithm to realize the automated and efficient
extraction of statistical features of power users’ electricity consumption data.
FRESH [24] is a feature engineering framework for time series classification and regression
tasks, and its core idea is to screen the features that are significantly correlated with the target

TS-Fresh is a Python library for implementing the FRESH algorithm, which replaces the
traditional manual feature engineering with systematic algorithms to automatically extract
hundreds of statistical features, and filter the features that are strongly correlated with the target
variables through hypothesis tests. Through the TS-Fresh filtering mechanism, 777 temporal

784 Yin et al.: Electricity Theft Detection Method
Based on CNN-Mamba and Multi-Dimensional Temporal Feature Cross-Enhancement
5. Conclusion
This paper proposes a method that for the first time combines a CNN-Mamba hybrid model
with multi-dimensional temporal feature cross-attention fusion, constructing a framework of
“multi-scale local sensing-global temporal modeling-heterogeneous feature fusion” for
electricity theft detection. Unlike the traditional single-dimensional approach, thi

, constructing a framework of
“multi-scale local sensing-global temporal modeling-heterogeneous feature fusion” for
electricity theft detection. Unlike the traditional single-dimensional approach, this method
simultaneously models the depth of spatio-temporal features of time series and the global
semantic characterization of statistical features, and breaks through the performance
bottleneck of the traditional static fusion method through the theory of dynamic fusion of
multidimensional time series features. Through ablation and comparative experiments, along
with a series of analytical results, it has been collectively confirmed that the proposed
framework enables reliable and efficient electricity theft detection. Its performance
improvements translate into tangible benefits for grid operation and reduced losses. Future
work may integrate the federated learning framework to achieve dynamic optimization while
ensuring privacy protection.
